title: Basic Wire Welding Technique and Shutdown Procedure
summary: This section covers the fundamental MIG wire welding technique including bead types, gun angles, and contact tip distance. It also details the proper shutdown, cooling, and wire/gas storage steps after welding is complete. Duty cycle guidelines and thermal overload behavior are also addressed.
key_facts: A stringer bead is made by drawing wire in a steady straight line.
A weave bead is made by drawing wire back and forth across the joint.
Butt welds require a 90-degree wire angle into the joint.
Fillet welds require a 45-degree wire angle into the joint.
Solid wire with shielding gas uses a push angle of 0-15 degrees away from weld direction.
Flux-cored wire without gas uses a drag angle of 0-15 degrees toward weld direction.
Contact Tip to Work Distance (CTWD) must remain within 1/2 inch of the work surface.
At 120 VAC, duty cycle is 40% use at 100 A over 10 continuous minutes.
At 240 VAC, duty cycle is 25% use at 200 A over 10 continuous minutes.
If overheated, the LCD warning screen appears and the unit automatically shuts down.
Allow the Welder to cool with the Power Switch on so the internal fan aids cooling.
When respooling wire, hold it securely as it tends to unravel once it clears the feeder.
procedure_steps: 1. Press and hold Trigger and contact the area to be welded with electrode wire to ignite arc.
2. For a narrow weld, draw wire in a steady straight line (stringer bead); for a wider weld, draw wire back and forth across the joint (weave bead).
3. Direct the welding wire straight into the joint: 90° for butt welds, 45° for fillet welds.
4. For MIG welding with solid wire and shielding gas, tilt MIG Gun 0-15° away from the direction of welding (push angle).
5. When using flux-cored wire without shielding gas, tilt MIG Gun 0-15° in the direction of welding (drag angle).
6. Keep the Contact Tip within 1/2 inch of the work surface (CTWD).
7. After welding a test weld on scrap for a few seconds, stop and examine the weld; make adjustments as needed and follow duty cycle guidelines.
8. When welding is complete, set the MIG Gun down on a heat-proof, electrically non-conductive surface and turn the Power Switch OFF.
9. Allow Welder to cool down, then unplug the Power Cord.
10. Remove Ground Clamp from workpiece or table and disconnect MIG Gun.
11. Respool wire by clipping wire, removing gas nozzle/contact tip, releasing Idler Arm on Wire Feed mechanism, and rotating Wire Spool counterclockwise while securely holding the wire.
12. MIG ONLY: Close shielding gas cylinder valve securely, remove Regulator and replace cap, disconnect Gas Hose from Welder, and store and secure gas cylinder.
warnings: CAUTION! Weld will be hot, do not touch.
Should this occur, rest the MIG Gun on an electrically non-conductive, heat‑proof surface, such as a concrete slab, away from the ground clamp.
Be sure to securely hold wire as it is being respooled because the end of wire has a tendency to quickly unravel once it clears the wire feeder.
When normal operation resumes, use shorter welding periods and longer rest periods to help prevent needless wear.
query_terms: contact tip to work distance CTWD
